title: The Market Inside the Market: Odd-Lot Quotes
abstract: We show current market practices relating to odd-lot quotes create a large “inside” market where better prices routinely exist relative to the National Best Bid or Offer. We show that odd-lot quotes play a price discovery role, and these quotes provide valuable information to traders with access to proprietary data feeds. Using a XGBoost machine learning algorithm that uses odd-lot data to predict future prices, we demonstrate a simple and profitable trading strategy. We argue the SEC’s proposed round-lot redefinition reduces—but does not eliminate—the high incidence of superior odd-lot quotes within the NBBO.

author.Justin McCrary: Columbia University Law School and National Bureau of Economic Research (NBER),USA
author.Maureen O’Hara*: Johnson College of Business, Cornell University,USA

article.1 A Short Primer on Odd-Lot Quotes:
JEL G12 - Asset Pricing; Trading volume; Bond Interest Rates G14 - Information and Market Efficiency; Event Studies; Insider Trading G28 - Government Policy and Regulation Issue Section: Article Editor: Itay Goldstein Itay Goldstein Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

The National Best Bid or Offer (NBBO) establishes the “best” price to buy (the offer) or sell (the bid) every U.S.-listed equity security at each point in time during the trading day, except that it doesn’t.1The NBBO only reports quotes based on “round lots” (100 share orders), so quotes arising from smaller orders (called odd lots) are not generally included. In 1975 when the system was implemented, this omission was largely irrelevant but that is no longer the case. The rise of algorithmic trading which chops orders into smaller pieces, the advent of very high-priced stocks (in our sample period a round lot of AMZN costs over $330,000; GOOG approximately $280,000), and the renaissance of retail traders have all made odd-lot quotes far more numerous. As a result, there is now a “market inside the market,” where better quotes routinely exist compared to those disseminated as the NBBO.2

In this paper we investigate the odd-lot quotes that populate this inside market. For some stocks we show that odd-lot quotes play a minor role, but this is not the case for others. In our sample period, 60% of the time a better odd-lot bid was available for Amazon, 54% of the time for Google, 53% of the time for Tesla, and over 25% of the time for Facebook. How important are these odd-lot quotes in general and what do they imply for liquidity and price discovery in equity trading markets? Do changes in odd-lot quote midpoints, for example, provide information for subsequent movements in the NBBO midpoint? Equally important are the implications for information asymmetries between market participants. Odd-lot quotes are not visible to those watching the “tape,” but they are visible to those purchasing the proprietary data feeds from each of the exchanges. How much of an advantage does knowing this information convey about future price movements relative to knowing only the national “pretty good” bid or offer? Finally, we address a policy question: will changing the size of what constitutes a round lot as proposed by the SEC remove the issues posed by the inside market?

To address these questions, we use a new data set that contains virtually every message, quote and trade on the 16 U.S. stock exchanges during our January-March 2021 sample period. These data, provided to us by LSEG, are the same data the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) uses for its high frequency market surveillance platform called “MIDAS.” We note at the outset that the scale of these data poses major challenges for doing empirical research. Several of our tables, for example, involve 32 billion data points, an astonishing number given the length of our sample period. But such is the nature of today’s high frequency markets, and these data give us the rare ability to see the entire market, both inside and out. Given such ‘big data’, we use machine learning techniques to evaluate the information content of odd-lot information, allowing us to assess the value of knowing the rich montage of odd-lot information available (or not available) to some market participants.

Our research provides a number of new results on this inside market. We divide our sample stocks into 5 price-based buckets, with stocks in group 1 having prices $20.00 or less, up to group 5 stocks with prices $250.00 and higher. We find that traders submit odd-lot quotes for all stock groups and that the rate of odd-lot orders ranges from 5.7% of all submitted orders for fewer than 500 shares in group 1 to 46.9% of all such orders in group 5. The odd-lot spread is smaller (i.e., better) than the spread of the NBBO for all stock groups, and this advantage increases with stock price, reaching on average 26% better for group 5 stocks. Perhaps most intriguing are our results on the incidence of superior odd-lot quotes relative to the NBBO. While for the lower price stocks this is only the case an average 5.2% of the time on the bid, this incidence reaches almost 30% for group 4 stocks and it averages 42% for our highest price group. (Similar figures apply to the offer.) We use regression analysis to predict the incidence of such superior pricing, with our results highlighting the important interactions between variables such as price, NBBO spreads, volatility, prior returns, and volume.

But does any of this really matter? Odd-lot quotes are for small amounts so even in large numbers they could still have little impact on the market as a whole. We address this issue in four ways. First, we focus on depth and ask: can any meaningful trade size be executed inside the spread? We aggregate all odd-lot orders that are inside the NBBO to get a measure of odd-lot depth relative to depth displayed at the NBBO at each point in time. At first glance, our results suggest that the answer is no for many stocks: for the three lowest-priced groups the average inside depth is less than 4% of the depth displayed at the NBBO. But this result is driven by the fact that if spreads are at one tick then there can be no inside odd-lot quotes. Conditioning on superior odd-lot quotes, the picture is very different, with inside depth across our stock groups averaging almost 15% of the displayed NBBO depth. Moreover, our regressions show how this odd-lot depth is affected by other market variables, providing a road map for smart trading algorithms to exploit this less visible depth. From a trade execution perspective, our results show that odd-lot orders can play an important role, particularly for higher priced stocks.

Second, we investigate what role, if any, odd-lot quotes play in the price discovery process. As we will explain later, odd-lot quotes can result from aggressive order strategies, but they also can emerge from simple market trading dynamics. We hypothesize that these origins can have very different implications for subsequent price movements, and we test these conjectures by examining whether changes in the odd-lot midpoint quote predict subsequent movements in the SIP midpoint quote. Our results that midpoint odd-lot spreads are predictive of future NBBO spread midpoints demonstrates a hitherto unrecognized role of the inside market.

Third, we ask about the information content of these odd-lot orders for broader price dynamics. In particular, how well do odd-lot metrics predict whether a stock’s price will be higher in one minute? This issue is of particular importance given that only some traders purchase proprietary data feeds for all exchanges, while others rely on information from the two Securities Information Processors (the “SIPs”) that are responsible for collecting and disseminating exchanges’ best bids and offers throughout the trading day. Relative to subscribing to all exchanges’ proprietary data feeds, acquiring data from the SIPs is simple and cheap, but the SIP data only include round-lot quotations from the exchanges.

To explore the informational advantage of observing odd-lot quotes from the proprietary data feeds, we focus on group 1 stocks (the lowest price group) and group 5 stocks (stock prices at or above $250.00) and create two predictive models. We postulate a simple “SIP model” based on data from the SIP feed and a rival “OL model” that adds odd-lot quotation data from the proprietary feeds. We use regression analysis to investigate whether quotation data can explain the log difference between the volume-weighted average price between minutetand minutet+ 1 (VWAPt+1⁠) and the SIP midpoint observed at minutet. We find strong evidence that odd-lot quotes have information distinct from that in the SIP data. While most SIP variables are not significant, OL spreads and OL imbalance show strong positive associations with VWAP returns. Moreover, the OL model’sR-squared is nearly six times that of the SIP model, although the overall fit of both models remains low.

Given the massive size of our data, we turn to supervised machine learning—in particular, XGBoost, a gradient boosting decision tree algorithm—to investigate further how well the SIP model and OL model predict whether the label VWAP through timet+ 1 is greater than the SIP midpoint at timetgiven features at timet. We find strong predictability for both the SIP and OL models, but particularly so for the OL model, with accuracy, precision, and recall metrics of 55%, 56%, and 58%, respectively. We use the Shapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) method to explore why the odd-lot data provide such superior performance and to establish the differential predictive ability of particular data features.

Finally, can the predictions of the XGBoost algorithm provide profitable trading strategies? Based on a very simple trading strategy, the short answer is yes. When the model predicts with at least 50% probability a positive label (i.e., the stock price going up), we calculate the round-trip trading profit (net of the effective half spread calculated over all stock-days in our test data) for a purchase at the beginning of a stock minute and a sale at VWAPt+1⁠. Two points here are particularly salient. One is that trading costs overwhelm the profit potential of this simple strategy for both the SIP and OL models. But raising the probability threshold of a positive label to 65% shows a different story: the SIP model provides negligible returns while the OL model produces average daily returns of 1.36% on reasonable volume. Indeed, we find positive returns to the OL model at thresholds as high as 90%, whereas the SIP model has no positive predictions (and thus 0% returns) at thresholds higher than 75%. We interpret these results as demonstrating that odd-lot data are informative and valuable.

Overall, our results underscore that the “market inside the market” is important enough that analysis using information only on round-lot quotes has the potential to lead to erroneous conclusions, especially for high-priced stocks. The issues surrounding odd-lots, as well as broader issues connected with what data in general should be visible to the market, have attracted extensive industry and regulator attention.3Many of these issues are beyond our focus here, but questions of whether and how odd-lot quotes should be reported as “core,” publicly available data are certainly germane. As part of its new rules (“Reg NMS II”), theSEC (2020b) recently adopted changes to the definition of what constitutes a round lot. For stocks with prices at or below $250.00, a round lot stays at 100 shares; for stock prices greater than $250.00 and up to $1,000.00, a round lot would be 40 shares; for stocks above $1,000.00 up to $10,000.00, 10 shares; and for stocks above that level, one share. Under this definition, and when the rules are actually implemented, the number of odd-lot quotes will be reduced, but how well will this fix the problems posed by odd-lot quotes?

We perform a counterfactual analysis to examine how this proposed round-lot definition would have changed odd-lot incidence and depth in our January to March 2021 sample period.4Our analysis shows that for our sample stocks in the 40-share category, the incidence of superior odd-lot quotes (relative to the NBBO using the new round-lot definitions) would fall by approximately 4.9%. But even with this new definition, there is a better odd-lot bid 35.5% of the time and a better odd lot ask 33.5% of the time. For sample stocks falling into the 10-share category, the improvement is greater, with the incidence falling approximately 26.7% of the time. Here, again, though, the inside market remains large: the odd-lot bid (ask) is better 37.0% (35.3%) of the time. We discuss some of the many interesting dimensions of this policy, and the odd lot problem in general, further in the conclusion.

Our paper joins a small academic literature looking at odd-lot equity trading and a much larger literature considering equity market structure.5Battalio, Corwin, and Jennings (2017)first looked at odd-lot quoting in a sample of 10 stocks using one week of data. Other research examining odd-lot trading areO’Hara, Yao, and Ye (2014), who establish the importance of odd-lot trades and their exclusion from the consolidated tape and TAQ data, andBartlett (2021), who examines whether odd-lot trades receive best execution. Market structure research addresses a wide range of topics including tick and lot size (see, e.g.,Buti et al. 2022;O’Hara, Saar, and Zhou 2018;Bartlett and McCrary 2020;Chao, Yao, and Ye 2019;Li and Ye 2023); latency (Brogaard et al. 2015;Bartlett and McCrary 2019), and routing decisions (Battalio, Corwin, and Jennings 2016;Cimon 2021). Our work is most closely related to the literature examining proprietary data feeds (Cespa and Foucault 2014;Easley, O’Hara, and Yang 2016). As we will show here, traders with access to proprietary data on odd lots have a significant advantage over traders relying on the SIP data, raising important questions of information asymmetry connected to the inside market. These issues are at the heart of Reg NMS II, which sets out wide-ranging changes to the collection and dissemination of market data. Our analysis suggests that the proposed definition of a round lot in Reg NMS II may reduce, but not substantially ameliorate, the problems posed by the inside market.

Our research also contributes to the growing literature using big data for finance research.6Chinco, Clark-Joseph, and Ye (2018)apply LASSO techniques to make 1-minute-ahead equity return forecasts; Isil et al. (2021) consider how well machine learning algorithms assess director performance;Philip (2020)uses reinforcement learning to estimate the permanent price impact of a trade;Rossi and Utkus (2020)use machine learning to investigate winners and losers in robo investing;Gu, Kelly, and Xiu (2020)apply multiple machine learning regression algorithms in asset pricing; andEasley et al. (2021)apply random forest to investigate the predictive power of microstructure variables. Our work contributes to this literature by showing how information can be extracted from large data sets using XGBoost and how these results can be interpreted using SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations), an “explainable ML” method based on game theoretic Shapley values. Our analysis provides sharp results on the value of knowing odd-lot data in equity markets.
The “inside market” we study is populated with limit orders to buy or sell in quantities fewer than 100 shares, all at prices better than the current quotes.7These odd-lot quotes arise for a variety of reasons in the market, and they are treated differently than round-lot quotes with respect to some market rules. As useful context for our analysis, this section sets out some important features of these orders, why such orders are not homogeneous, and how the origins of these orders might be expected to affect their role in liquidity and price discovery. We also discuss the role differential information plays with respect to odd-lot quotes in market behavior.

Because the national market system was designed for round-lot trading, odd-lot orders do not receive the same protections provided to round-lot orders. In particular, the best-priced round-lot order posted on an exchange is “protected” under Rule 611 of Regulation NMS, which means that no trading venue can execute a marketable order at a price that is worse than the price of the protected round-lot quote.8This “trade through” protection increases the likelihood that marketable orders will be routed to the best-round lot quotes for execution. However, the rule does not apply to odd lots, meaning that odd-lot quotes with better prices can be disadvantaged with respect to execution priority.

Odd-lot quotes are also excluded from the definition of a “bid” or “offer” under Regulation NMS, meaning that an odd-lot quote cannot set an exchange’s best bid or offer (BBO) and will not be published by the two securities information processors (SIPs) responsible for administering the consolidated trade and quote system. Consequently, traders seeking liquidity may not be aware of better odd-lot liquidity available across the various exchanges (for more discussion, seeBartlett 2021). That said, exchanges are permitted to define their BBOs by aggregating their most aggressively priced orders up to 100 shares and using the price of the least aggressive order; for instance, if an exchange’s top-four bids are 50 shares priced at $10.10, 25 shares priced at $10.05, 25 shares priced at $10.00, and 100 shares priced at $9.95, the exchange can display to the SIPs a best bid for 100 shares priced at $10.00. While this displayed bid for 100 shares at $10.00 could also establish the NBB, the SIP data would not display the fact that there were two better priced odd-lot bids resting on the exchange. During our sample period, all exchanges adopted this procedure; therefore, the “market inside a market” we study exists even though exchanges aggregate odd-lot quotes in this fashion to determine their BBOs.

Odd-lot quotes inside the NBBO spread arise for two main reasons: trading strategies and “crumbling quotes.” Turning first to trading strategies, the advent of high-priced stocks has made trading in a round lot infeasible for many retail traders, so entering an odd-lot limit order provides access to these equities. Why retail traders choose to submit such orders within the NBBO spread is unclear, but one explanation may simply be to transact at a better price. To the extent these retail orders are from uniformed traders, we would expect them to contain little or no information for future price movements. Institutional traders submit odd-lot orders to the inside market for a variety of reasons. Algorithmic trading as practiced by institutional traders to reduce price impact initially eschewed odd-lot orders, but that is now no longer the case. Algorithms routinely submit small (often one-share) orders within the spread to determine whether there is liquidity resting there arising from either odd-lot orders or hidden orders, although these orders are generally market orders rather than limit orders.9A more compelling reason to submit an odd-lot limit order to the inside market is to take advantage of the reduced transparency of such orders. Because the inside market is not reported in the consolidated feed (the SIP data), orders submitted there are less visible to the market as a whole. If such odd-lot limit orders are from informed traders, then these orders may be informative of future price movements, an issue we address in Section 5.

Odd-lot limit orders inside the spread can also arise mechanistically through a process we describe as crumbling quotes. Suppose, for example, that a marketable order to sell executes against the National Best Bid (NBB). If the sell order is for 250 shares and the bid is for 300 shares, the remaining 50 shares at the bid are now less than a round lot and so become an odd-lot order. The NBB could also “crumble” into an odd-lot bid if orders that constitute the NBB are cancelled. As the NBB declines to the next-highest round-lot bid, the process leaves in place better-priced odd-lot bids compared to the new, lower NBB. As above, the reverse logic applies to the NBO: marketable orders to buy can cause the NBO to crumble in size, leaving behind better-priced odd-lot offers relative to the new, higher-priced NBO. These odd-lot-induced market dynamics are likely to have very different implications for subsequent price and quote movements than those arising from the odd-lot trading strategies discussed above. We will investigate these price dynamics in Section 4.

A particularly important feature of the inside market is the role played by differential information. As noted earlier, the prices and sizes of odd-lot quotes in the inside market are not part of the quote montage and so are not visible to market participants relying on SIP data. But this information is available on the proprietary data feeds sold by the exchanges. TheSEC (2020c) has called attention to the expense of these proprietary feeds noting that this results in a “broken and inequitable system” that serves “very few investors well.” One might expect that an indirect measure of the value of the odd-lot information could be inferred by the fees charged for these data, but this simple intuition is thwarted by the complexity of the data market. Each exchange charges for a variety of bundled data products so just buying the odd-lot data in the inside market is not really feasible. Moreover, these charges include access fees, user fees, nondisplay fees, and redistribution fees, to say nothing of the costs of the data servers and other software and hardware needed to handle data sets of this size.10While prices for data vary across the 16 exchanges, seeing the inside market would require access to feeds from all of them (or to a data aggregator who in turn would pass on these fees to its customers). The reality is that such data acquisition is only feasible for a subset of market participants, setting the stage for our analysis of what you are missing when you cannot see the inside market.
article.2 Data:
Our analyses primarily rely on a set of proprietary data feeds obtained from LSEG, the data vendor that supplies to the SEC the microdata underlying the MIDAS system.11For recent years, these data capture all (nonhidden) trade and quote messages for all exchanges in the United States. There are two major advantages of the LSEG data relative to the Trade and Quote (TAQ) data used in traditional market microstructure analyses. First, it has information on odd-lot quotes. Second, it has complete order book information for each exchange. In contrast, TAQ data have only round-lot quote information and top-of-book quote information for each exchange. These twin advantages allow us to speak to the extent to which the best odd-lot quotes are inside the NBBO, as well as how much total odd-lot quotation volume rests inside the NBBO.

Our sample encompasses trading days from January 4, 2021, to March 31, 2021. We focus on a recent time period because odd lots are increasingly prevalent, so studying them is easier in more recent data.12The interface for the LSEG data is query-driven, so the next step in our study design is to pick a set of stocks to query. A core consideration for us is liquidity. Part of our study is a set of prediction models seeking to understand the extent to which odd-lot quote activity is predictive of future changes to the VWAP. Consequently, we need observations on VWAP throughout the trading day, and so our focus will be on stocks with sufficient liquidity for this to be the case.

To determine which stocks might be sufficiently liquid, we use the TAQ trade data from December 2020, just before our analysis period.13For each stock-day in December, we compute the largest gap in time between two consecutive trades during the trading day.14For each stock, we take the maximum of those maxima, and restrict attention to the set of stocks for which that maximum gap is less than 5 minutes. We additionally require that a stock trade after December 31, 2020. This leads to a set ofn=1,736stocks, which we refer to as our “study stocks.”15These are by design relatively more liquid than other stocks. They contain nearly all (501 of 505) of the stocks composing the S&P 500 during our sample period.

As noted inO’Hara et al. (2014), odd lots are particularly common among expensive stocks. We divide our set of 1,736 stocks into five groups based on the average VWAP observed across full trading days in December 2020. We use cut points of $20, $50, $100, and $250. To contextualize our analysis,Figure 1shows the approximately 5-year time series of the fraction of trades that are odd for our set of 1,736 stocks (black line), as well the time series for each of the five groups (gray lines). The figure shows that over time this fraction has risen for all five groups, but the levels are notably different. Speaking generally, price roughly orders the extent of odd lots. For example, for group 1, the fraction of trades that are odd rises from just over 15% to 40% and for group 5, the fraction rises from 40% to 83%.

Next, we query the LSEG system for information on these stocks during our sample period.16Our first set of queries relates to our analysis of the size of individual messages to add liquidity. For this analysis, we pull every message to add liquidity across the 1,736 study stocks during the sample period. The second set of queries focuses on pulling data for the full order book each day for the study stocks at the beginning of each minute in our study period. In particular, we examine the entire order book at 390 snapshots in time—once at the beginning of each minute between 9:30 a.m. and 4 p.m.—each day. Economizing on analysis in this way is efficient: although this discards much of the data, for our 1,736 study stocks during the 61 trading days of our sample period, examining 390 snapshots during the day still involves analysis of over 41 million observations. Variables included are the set of 20 best bids (price and size), 20 best offers (price and size), prevailing NBBO (price and size), and VWAP during the ensuing minute.

Figure 2provides an example of how the top of the book looks to a consumer of the SIP/TAQ data relative to a consumer of the exchanges’ proprietary data feeds using Amazon common stock at 13:25:00 on March 31, 2021. At the beginning of this minute, the TAQ data (panel A) indicate that the national best bid (NBB) stood at $3,110.67 due to a round-lot bid at this price on both Nasdaq and MIAX, and the national best offer (NBO) stood at $3,113.91 due to a single round-lot order to sell on Nasdaq. Thus, the quoted spread in panel A is $3.24.

In contrast, as shown in panel B, a consumer of the exchanges’ proprietary data feeds would see very different prices due to the presence of odd-lot quotes. Specifically, panel B shows that the best bid price at this time was $3,112.59 ($1.92 higher than the NBB) due to an order to buy 36 shares at this price on BATS Z and an order to buy one share at this price on Nasdaq. Panel B also shows that the best offer price at this time was $3,113.19 ($0.72 less than the NBO) due to an offer to sell 2 shares at this price on Arca. Overall, the quoted spread for these odd-lot orders is just $0.60, or less than one-fifth of the quoted spread of the NBBO.

Panel B also highlights the large number of odd-lot quotes resting at superior prices to the NBBO. For instance, while the SIP/TAQ data show total bid depth of 200 shares, or approximately $622,000, the LSEG data show that the aggregate depth of superior priced bids was 405 shares, or approximately $1.3 million. On the offer, the SIP/TAQ data show total offer depth of 100 shares, or $311,391, while the LSEG data show aggregate depth of superior priced offers amounting to 131 shares, or approximately $408,000. Moreover, these superior priced odd-lot quotes exist despite the fact that all exchanges during this time period set their BBOs by aggregating their most aggressively priced orders up to 100 shares and using the price of the least aggressive order. Despite the number of odd-lot quotes across all exchanges, no single exchange had better-priced odd-lot quotes that aggregated to more than 99 shares; therefore, each exchange’s BBO was established only by round-lot orders.

Lastly, panel B illustrates how the exclusion of odd-lot quotes in the SIP/TAQ data can overlook additional depth at the NBBO. In panel B, an order to buy 50 shares at the NBB price rested on Arca but was not included in the depth at the NBB in panel A. The SIP/TAQ data in panel A also exclude orders to sell shares at the NBO on BATS Y and BATS Z for 10 shares and 1 share, respectively.

3.1 Summary statistics

Table 1summarizes our study stocks during the sample period. We divide our 1,736 study stocks into five groups defined by: group 1 (VWAP ≤$20), group 2 ($20 <VWAP ≤$50), group 3 ($50 <VWAP ≤$100), group 4 ($100 <VWAP ≤$250), and group 5 (VWAP >$250). Each group has 100 or more stocks, but group 1 (496) has more stocks than group 5 (109). Unsurprisingly, the VWAP is lowest for group 1 and highest for group 5. For groups 1–4, there is little difference between the mean and median VWAP, but for group 5, where the price range for VWAP is unbounded, there is notable skew to the right in the distribution: median VWAP is $353.32, but mean VWAP is $507.06.

We call the best (highest) bid displayed by the SIPs the SIP bid, and we call the best bid displayed across exchanges’ proprietary data feeds the OL Bid (and similarly for theSIP offerandOL offer). TheSIP bidandSIP offertogether constitute the NBBO, and similarly theOL bidandOL offercompose the OL NBBO. TheSIP spread=SIP offer—SIP bid; theOL spread=OL offer—OL bid.17

TheSIP spreadis quite narrow for group 1 and larger for other groups, ranging from roughly one to two cents for group 1 to almost a dollar for group 5.Table 1shows that the averageSIP spreadis 2.5 cents for group 1, 6.6 cents for group 2, 13.4 cents for group 3, 24.8 cents for group 4, and 91.8 cents for group 5 (with median SIP spreads somewhat tighter). The Table also shows that the averageOL spreadfor group 1 is 12% tighter than theSIP spread, but for group 5 it is over 26% tighter than theSIP spread. Thus, better prices can be found in the inside market across our spectrum of stocks.

TheOL spreadbeing tighter than theSIP spreaddoes not necessarily say much about whether there is meaningful liquidity among odd lots. To assess the extent to which there is significant odd-lot depth inside the NBBO, we next compare the depth inside the NBBO to the depth at the NBBO. To do so, we look at three metrics: the dollar value at the SIP bid (“Depth at top of SIP bid”); the dollar value at the OL Bid (“Depth at Top of OL bid”); and the cumulative dollar value on offer at prices strictly better than the SIP bid, all the way up to the OL Bid (“Total depth at OL bids”). If there are no odd lots better than the SIP bid, then Depth at top of OL bid is set to missing, and similarly for Total depth at OL bids. Thus, theTable 1statistics for these measures indicate the amount of depth inside the SIP bid when the OL Bid is better than the SIP bid. These metrics are constructed analogously on the offer side.18

The dollar value available at the SIP bid (“Depth at top of SIP bid”) exhibits a long right-hand tail, so medians are a more reliable measure of the distribution’s spread. Medians for groups 1 through 3 are approximately $20,000 to $30,000. For groups 4 and 5, median Depth at the Top of the SIP bid rises to $50,000 and $100,000, respectively. TheSIP offershows a similar cross-group pattern as the SIP bid, with theSIP offerrising from a little over $20,000 for group 1 to just over $100,000 for group 5. As expected, the dollar value on offer at the OL Bid is notably smaller than the dollar value on offer at the SIP Bid, and this pattern is stronger for group 1 than for the more expensive groups. For example, for group 1, median Depth at Top of OL Bid is under 2% of the dollar value associated with Depth at top of SIP bid, but this depth is nearly 7% for group 5.

Taking account of all odds lots between the SIP bid and the OL Bid has little effect for groups 1 and 2, but roughly doubles the above figures for group 5. For group 1, the median Total depth at OL bids is 2% of the dollar value associated with Depth at top of SIP bid, for group 2 it is 5%, for group 3 it is 8%, for group 4 it is 10%, and for group 5 it is almost 14%. For group 5, in particular, this is meaningful volume on offer inside of the NBBO. While these figures were specific to the bid side, the numbers inTable 1regarding the offer side are similar quantitatively, and certainly convey the same qualitative conclusions.

To develop further intuition, we examine the distribution of order sizes.Figure 3provides a histogram of order size (in bins) for our five groups of stock. The size bins are for 1–49 shares, 50–99 shares, 100–199 shares, 200–299 shares, 300–399 shares, 400–499 shares, and 500 or more shares.19Turning to the results, for group 1 there is very little mass below 100 shares.Table 2quantifies this result: the first two bins are approximately 6% of the distribution for group 1. In contrast, approximately 60% of the distribution is 100–199 shares. Roughly 20% of the distribution is for 200–499 shares, and 14% are for 500 or more shares. These large orders are notable in part because for the other four groups, orders of that size are unusual. Turning to group 5—a large 42% of orders are for 1–49 shares. Orders for 50–99 shares are also prevalent, around 5% of the total. A smaller portion—about half—of orders are for 100–199 shares, and there are few orders for 200 or more shares. The final row ofTable 2summarizes across all groups. This shows that for all 1,736 of our study stocks, roughly 16% of orders adding liquidity are odd. Some 64% of such orders are for 100–199 shares; the fraction falls to approximately 8% for 200–299, 3% for 300–399, 2% for 400–499, before rising to nearly 7% for over 500.

3.2 Do odd-lot quotes provide superior pricing?

So far, we have established that (a) odd-lots quotes are a common form of liquidity provision in the market today, (b) they are unevenly distributed across stocks, but are particularly prominent among more expensive stocks, and (c) there is nonnegligible volume available at odd lots with superior pricing to the NBBO, particularly for those same expensive stocks that attract odd-lot liquidity in the first place.

3.2.1 The incidence of odd-lot pricing

We now turn to examining four related questions: (1) How often do odd-lot quotes offer better prices than those at the NBBO? (2) On a minute-by-minute basis, what characteristics are associated with odd-lot prices being better than the NBBO? (3) How much odd-lot depth is there relative to depth at the NBBO? (4) On a minute-by-minute basis, what characteristics are associated with high levels of better-priced odd-lot depth relative to depth at the NBBO?

We answer the first question in a simple but direct way inTable 3. For each stock day, we compute the fraction of the 390 end-of-minute observations where the OL NBBO is better than the SIP NBBO (“Superior Odd-Lot Pricing”) and then examine the distribution of those stock-day fractions within our sample. We present results separately for bids (panel A) and offers (panel B), and as results are similar we focus on the bid-side results. The average stock-day for stocks in group 1 has Superior Odd-Lot Pricing about 5% of the time. The distribution exhibits strong right-hand skew: the median is far below the mean at 1%, the 25th percentile is well below 1%, but the 75th percentile is a high 8%. Unsurprisingly given earlier results, the distribution of Superior Odd-Lot Pricing shifts strongly to the right as you move from group 1 to group 5. For example, the mean Superior Odd-Lot Pricing moves from 5% for group 1, to 14% for group 2, to 22% for group 3, to 30% for group 4, to fully 42% for group 5. For at least 27 stocks (those in the 75th percentile or above for the 109 group 5 stocks) nearly half the trading day (46.7%) goes by where the OL bid is better than the SIP bid.20

Overall, the regressions reported inTable 4are modestly, but not overwhelmingly predictive: across the four columns, theR2ranges from .13 to .15 or so. Focusing attention on column 1 and considering first the effect of theSIP spread, the coefficient for log spread is about one-tenth. The statistical significance is high (with a t-ratio of over 50).21However, statistical significance does not necessarily speak to economic significance. To interpret the coefficient, consider the following: suppose the spread increased by 10% and note that the coefficient is about one-tenth. Then the predicted increase in the probability that the OL Bid would be better than the SIP bid is 0.01, that is, one percentage point.22

The coefficient for log price is about one-tenth the magnitude of the coefficient for log spread and of the opposite sign, indicating that, after controlling for log spread, an increase in price is associated with a decrease in the chances that the OL Bid is better than the SIP bid. Despite the large sample size, however, the effect is statistically insignificant. In terms of interpreting the magnitude, a 10% increase in price is associated with a reduced predicted probability that the OL Bid would be better than the SIP bid of about 0.0002, that is, two hundredths of a percentage point. Turning to the effects of volume, we see that the effects are statistically significant at conventional levels, and somewhat larger in magnitude than those for price, but still small in economic terms. For example, a 10% increase in volume is predicted to lead to a reduced probability that the OL Bid would be better than the SIP bid of about five hundredths of a percentage point.

On the other hand, return and volatility have somewhat larger effects.23Both are measured in percentage points (i.e., the actual return×100⁠); therefore, a 10% return in the stock price over the previous minute is associated with a decrease of nearly 3.2 percentage points in the chances that the OL bid is better than the SIP bid. Similarly, a 10-percentage-point increase in stock-day daily volatility is associated with a decrease of nearly one-percentage-point in the chances that theOL bidis better than theSIP bid.

Turning to the results in column 2, we reach broadly similar conclusions, but with occasional sign changes. Higher spreads are associated with a better chance that the OL offer is inside theSIP offer, and controlling for spreads, higher prices are associated with lower chances that theOL offeris inside theSIP offer. Volume has a comparable statistical association with the offer side as with the bid side, but the magnitude of the effect is still small. In contrast, the sign of the effect for return is the opposite what it was for the bid side. As explored in more detail in Section 4, an increase in returns is consistent with the arrival of marketable buy orders, which may execute against only a portion of the NBO, leaving behind better priced odd-lot offers as the NBO increases in price. Conversely, for the bid side, a decrease in return is consistent with the arrival of marketable sell orders that may cause the NBB to decrease in price while leaving behind better priced odd-lot bids. Controlling for the other covariates in the model, daily volatility has the same (inverse) relation with the probability of better priced quotes on the offer as on the bid.

Columns 3 and 4 extend this analysis to contemplate interactions of each covariate with indicators for group. For log spread, the positive association with better odd-lot prices is generally greater for higher priced stocks, particularly for group 5 stocks. After controlling for log spreads, the interactions for log price do not appear to be very important, but for log volume, return, and volatility, there is substantial heterogeneity. Greater log volume and volatility are associated with a reduced probability of the OL NBBO bettering the SIP NBBO for both the bid and offer sides, and this negative association is of greater magnitude for higher priced stocks. Likewise for returns, the size of the negative (positive) association between returns and better priced odd-lot bids (offers) described above becomes greater the higher the price of the stock.

3.2.2 Odd-lot depth

We now address the third question outlined above, namely, the extent of better-priced odd-lot volume relative to the volume at the NBBO.Table 5starts by calculating for each stock-day the cumulative odd-lot volume on offer that is better than the round-lot volume displayed by the SIPs. We use two approaches to analyze this question. The first is an unconditional analysis, whereby when there are no odd lots better than the SIP NBBO, the odd-lot volume is considered to be zero. The second approach is a conditional analysis, where we ask: “When there are odd lots, how much odd lot volume is there relative to round lot volume?” Roughly speaking, the unconditional approach is “include the zeros” and the conditional approach is “truncate the zeros.” Panel A ofTable 5adopts the unconditional approach, and panel B ofTable 5adopts the conditional approach. Because of the positive skew in these fractions, the right side of the distribution is winsorized at 99%.

article.4 Do Odd-Lot Quotes Lead the NBBO?:
In this section, we explore the extent to which odd-lot orders resting “inside” the NBBO reflect the direction of future round-lot order prices at the top of the book. For instance, do odd-lot bids that are more aggressive than the NBB lead to the formation of a new, higher NBB at the top of the book in the near future? Or are these odd-lot bids largely fleeting such that they are orthogonal to the price of the NBB when it once again represents the best available bid? Where assets trade in multiple markets, an extensive literature has documented the tendency of one market to “lead” other markets with respect to future prices (see., e.g., Hasbrouck 1995). Our inquiry is similar in spirit to this literature, but our context requires us to take a different methodological approach for two reasons.

First, whereas the price of a security that trades in one market (e.g., NYSE) might be higher or lower than the price of that security in another market (e.g., BATSY), this symmetry of pricing is missing in the context of comparing the price of the next top-of-book NBB (NBO) relative to the best odd-lot bid (offer). For example, it is possible to examine whether the existence of a higher-priced odd-lot bid leads to a higher top-of-book NBB, but it is not possible to examine the reverse, that is, if there are no better-priced odd-lot bids, there are no superior odd-lot prices for the NBB to follow. The reverse challenge applies to comparing whether the next top-of-book NBO follows the price of a better priced odd-lot offer. This asymmetry leads us to focus on how the NBB or NBO evolves between the episodic odd-lot “runs” that displace the NBB or NBO from being the best price in the market.

Second, inquiring into whether odd-lot quotes lead the NBB or NBO requires grappling with why a better-priced odd-lot quote is created in the first place. As noted in Section 1, one reason that a better priced odd-lot bid might occur is because a trader posts an odd-lot bid that is more aggressive than the NBB. We refer to odd-lot runs that commence on this basis as New OL runs. In this context, it is straightforward to ask whether the next top-of-book NBB migrates toward the price of the new, aggressive odd-lot order. (The same logic applies to the NBO relative to a new lower, better-priced odd-lot offer.) However, better-priced odd-lot bids can also occur when a marketable order to sell executes against the NBB, causing the quote to “crumble.” We refer to odd-lot runs that commence on this basis as Residual OL runs.

We hypothesize that whether a better-priced odd-lot order predicts the price of the top-of-book NBB or NBO following an odd-lot run depends on whether the better-priced odd-lot order is part of a New OL run or a Residual OL run. In the context of the former, new aggressively priced orders may reflect first movers, suggesting they will predict where the round-lot NBB or NBO will be moving. In contrast, a Residual OL run can only arise because a marketable trade in the opposite direction has lowered (raised) the price of the NBB (NBO) or orders that formerly contributed to the NBB or NBO have been cancelled. In this latter setting, one might expect the residual odd-lot orders to be picked off by follow-on marketable orders and/or cancelled as the traders who posted the orders learn that the market is moving against them. As a result, when the NBB (NBO) is once again the best price in the market, it should be at a price that is lower (higher) than it was before the odd-lot run.

InFigure 4, we provide an example of each of these dynamics that occurred in the order book for Restoration Hardware (RH) (a group 5 stock) on January 4, 2021, at 10:51:43.5307. The figure plots the evolution of the top of the book for odd-lot orders as well as the NBB and NBO over the ensuing 2.05 seconds. At the beginning of this time period, the order book stood at 100 shares on the bid priced at $437.58 and 324 shares on the offer priced at $438.12, and there were no better priced odd-lot orders for either the bid or the offer. Seven microseconds later, an aggressive order to sell two shares at $437.85 is posted on Nasdaq, commencing an odd-lot run. Over the ensuing 900 microseconds, additional orders to sell at this price are posted on BATS (2 shares), EDGX (8 shares), and NYSE (2 shares), until a 100-share order at this price is placed at IEX, moving the NBO from $438.12 to $437.85. Thus, this sequence of orders is consistent with the emergence of a New OL run on the offer leading to a more aggressive price for the next top-of-book NBO.

Fig. 4 Illustration of odd-lot runs on the offer and bid for Restoration Hardware The figure plots the evolution of the top of the book for odd-lot orders as well as the NBB and NBO between 10:51:43.5307 and 10:51:46 on January 4, 2021. Open in new tab Download slide

A different story, however, applies to the NBB. Three microseconds after the arrival of the first odd-lot offer for $437.85, a marketable order to sell executes against one order at the NBB for 10 shares, leaving only 90 shares priced at $437.58. Immediately prior to this trade, the next best bid was for three shares at $437.21, followed by bids for 100 shares at $437.12. As a result, the NBB drops to $437.12, leaving an odd-lot bid for 90 shares at $437.58 as the best bid in the market. Roughly 100 microseconds later, another marketable order to sell executes against this order, such that the best odd-lot bid becomes an order for three shares at $437.21. Roughly 30,000 microseconds later, this order is cancelled, eliminating the last of the better priced odd-lot bids. Thus, this sequence of events is consistent with the emergence of a Residual OL run on the bid leading to a less aggressive top-of-book NBB as the residual odd-lot bids were filled or cancelled.

To examine whether these patterns are representative within our data, we collect from LSEG all updates to the BBOs for all 109 Group 5 stocks along with 100 randomly selected group 1 stocks. For each stock-day within these data, we examine the evolution of the NBB and NBO between odd-lot runs. Specifically, we focus on BBO updates when either the NBB or NBO ceased being the best price bid or offer due to the creation of better-priced odd-lot quotes, as was the case for the bid and the offer for RH inFigure 4. For each such BBO, we then classify whether the better-priced odd-lot bid or offer commenced a New OL run or a Residual OL run. Next, we record the price of the NBB (NBO) when it once again represents the best bid (offer) in the market following the odd-lot run, and we evaluate its price against the price of the NBB (NBO) prior to the odd-lot run, recording whether the second price was “better,” the “same,” or “worse” from the perspective of a trader seeking immediate liquidity. For instance, inFigure 4, we would classify the post-run NBB as “worse” than the prerun NBB, and we would classify the post-run NBO as “better” than the prerun NBO. Lastly, we record the duration of the odd-lot run.

In panel A ofTable 7, we present the total number of New OL runs and Residual OL runs on the bid, and the percentage of each that resulted in a post-run top-of-book NBB that was better, the same, or worse than the prerun top-of-book NBB. In panel B, we present the same analysis for the offer. In both panels, results are presented separately for group 1 and group 5 stocks. As expected, odd-lot runs of both types were much more common for group 5 stocks than for group 1 stocks. However, for both groups, results are consistent with the patterns shown inFigure 4. For the sample of group 1 stocks, column 1 of panel A reveals that when a New OL run commenced, the run ended with a top-of-book NBB or NBO that was better than the prerun NBB or NBO in over 60% of the instances. Likewise, for group 5 stocks, column 3 of panel A shows that the percentage for these odd-lot runs was 62.3% for the NBB and 63% for the NBO. Equally notable for New OL runs is the remarkably low rate at which the post-run NBB wasworsethan the prerun NBB; for group 1 stocks, New OL runs were followed by a worse NBB in just 1.8% of the instances, while for group 5 stocks the percentage was 2.9%. Columns 1 and 3 of panel B show that the results are nearly identical for the odd-lot runs on the offer.

Column 2 of both panels indicate that for group 1 stocks, Residual OL runs were predictive of a future top-of-book NBB or NBO having a price that is worse for a liquidity taker than the top-of-book NBB or NBO that existed immediately prior to the run. Specifically, for both the NBB and NBO in group 1 stocks, roughly 63% of these runs lead to a worse price for a liquidity taker executing at the NBB or NBO, while just 4% resulted in a better price. Likewise, the results for group 5 stocks are qualitatively similar, insofar that for both the NBB and NBO, roughly 49% of these runs led to a worse price for a liquidity taker executing at the NBB or NBO, while only 9.5% led to a better price. Inspection of the data reveal that the slightly different magnitudes of the group 1 and group 5 estimates is primarily due to differences in how a Residual OL run emerged. Specifically, when a Residual OL run occurred because the NBB (NBO) crumbled in size to become an odd lot, the next top-of-book NBB (NBO) declined (increased) in 63% (63%) of the instances for group 1 stocks but only 42% (41%) for group 5 stocks. In contrast, when the Residual OL run occurred because the NBB (NBO) decreased (increased) in price, the next top-of-book NBB (NBO) declined (increased) in 66% (64%) of these runs for group 1 stocks but declined in 73% (73%) of these runs for group 5 stocks.

We conclude from this analysis that better-priced odd-lot offers typically “lead” the NBB or NBO in the case of New OL runs, but not when they arise from Residual OL runs. In the latter setting, the evolution of the round-lot NBB or NBO tends to be in the opposite direction of the residual, better-priced odd-lot orders. Critically, however,Table 7also indicates that the time period over which these results occur is quite short; on average, the duration of odd-lot runs for both group 1 stocks and group 5 stocks are on the order of just a few seconds. In the following section, we investigate whether odd-lot orders might also have that capacity to predict future trade prices in a fashion that could be exploited in a simple trading strategy over a full minute.
article.5 Do Odd-Lot Quotes Predict Future Trade:
Given that odd-lot quotes are presently excluded from the SIP data, does access to odd-lot quotes provide traders with more valuable, actionable information than SIP data alone? The ability to predict the direction of the NBB or NBO, as shown in the prior section, is no doubt valuable to market makers. But the question we pose in this section is whether access to odd-lot quotation data can benefit traders more generally, relative to those traders who have access to only the SIP data. We approach this question in two steps. First, using both linear regression and machine learning techniques, we ask whether odd-lot quotes are predictive of future trade prices. Second, we ask whether odd-lot quotes provide traders with exploitable trading opportunities that are not available to traders using only the SIP data.

5.1 Setup

In designing our predictive models, we focus on the capacity of quotation data to predict prices over the 1-minute interval that follows the observation of an odd-lot quotation. In particular, because our data represent a snapshot of the quoting environment at the beginning of each minute throughout the trading day, we examine whether the quote data observed at the beginning of minutetare predictive of the volume-weighted average price observed through the commencement of minutet+ 1, which we refer to as VWAPt+1⁠. In our regression analysis, we focus on the extent to which quotation data can explain the log difference between VWAPt+1and the SIP midpoint observed att. In our machine learning analysis, we focus on whether the quote data can predict whether VWAPt+1is above the SIP midpoint att.

For our baseline “SIP model,” the quotation data include only the following data that are available from the SIPs (all observed at the commencement oft):

Note that many of these parameters build on our analysis from Section 4. For instance,Better bidandBetter offermay be indicative of a New OL run. However, because our inquiry is whether we can predict trades beyond just a few seconds, our primary interest is in utilizing odd-lot data that may be predictive of the arrival of marketable orders to buy or sell over the following minute. (In particular, the trading strategy we utilize below requires successfully predicting the arrival of marketable buy orders in order to be profitable). Thus, our focus in including these variables is in whether they represent latent buying or selling interest in the market at timet.25

Lastly, to account for the possibility that prices betweentandt+ 1 are influenced by prior trades, we also include as parameters in both models the natural logarithm of the volume of trades observed betweent−1andt, the natural logarithm of the cumulative volume of trades since the beginning of the trading day throught, and the log difference between the return on the volume-weighted average price during the minutet−1and the minutet.

Note that we make no claim that these parameters or the model specifications are the optimal set of parameters or specifications a trader would use. On the contrary, we purposefully keep the models parsimonious under the principle that, to the extent the odd-lot quotation data has meaningful informational content, this informational advantage should be apparent even in a simple predictive model. At the same time, we acknowledge that this approach places a considerable bias against a finding that quote data has informational content. In particular, our sample stocks represent very liquid exchange-traded equity securities, and our outcome of interest is observed over a full 60 seconds. Under these conditions, market efficiency suggests it should be difficult (if not impossible) to find that quote data observed at the beginning of a minute has the capacity to predict future prices.

5.2 Regression results

We first evaluate the capacity of the two models to explain the cross-sectional variation of our outcome of interest, the log difference between VWAPt+1and the SIP midpoint observed att. In light of the very different levels of odd-lot quotes across our five groups of stocks, we run the analysis separately for group 1 stocks (low levels of odd-lot quotes inside the NBBO) and group 5 stocks (high levels of odd-lot quotes inside the NBBO) to examine whether the informational content of odd-lot quotes varies by stock price.

The positive, significant coefficient for the prior minute’s VWAP return is consistent with the existence of an intra-day momentum effect. Turning to the quotation variables, the SIP model for group 1 reveals a negative association between spreads and returns, as one might expect, though the result becomes insignificant with stock fixed effects. The coefficient forSIP midpointsuggests a positive association with price and returns in column 1, but the association is reversed once we add stock fixed effects. In contrast, the estimates forSIP imbalanceand forPrior SIP MP Returnare both strongly significant and constant across both columns 1 and 3, indicating that strong relative bid depth and a negative return to the SIP midpoint over the past minute are associated with positive future VWAP returns.

Columns 2 and 4 similarly reveal a number of statistically significant associations between the odd-lot quote data and the ensuing minute’s VWAP returns, suggesting that the information content of odd lots is predictive. For example, the tightly estimated elasticity of 0.22 for the variableDifferenceimplies that a 10% increase in the OL midpoint relative to the SIP midpoint predicts a 2.2% increase in the VWAP return over the next minute. Similarly, while the coefficient estimate forSIP spreadremains negative, the estimate forOL Spreadis positive, indicating that odd-lot quotes (regardless of size) may have information that is distinct from that contained in the SIP data. Including odd-lot quote data in OL model also changes many of the coefficient estimates for variables in the SIP model. For instance, while the effect ofSIP midpointis slightly positive in column 1, its estimated effect becomes negative in column 2 whenOL midpointis included and the effect ofOL midpointis positive. Moreover, the statistical significance ofSIP midpointincreases somewhat.26

Despite these associations, however, the overall model fit is poor for both the SIP model and the OL model. Even with stock fixed effects, overallR-squared is just .003–.004 for both models. That the result holds across both models no doubt reflects the fact that, as shown previously, OL quotes rarely differ from SIP quotes for group 1 securities.

Turning to the results for group 5 stocks, the results shown in columns 5 through 8 are similar to those for group 1 stocks in both the SIP model and OL model in terms of statistically significant associations. The primary exceptions are that the past minute’s trading volume is no longer associated with future returns, and the signs forOL bid depthandOL offer depthflip for group 5 stocks compared to group 1 stocks. A more notable difference for group 5 stocks is with respect to the magnitude of the coefficients in columns 5 through 8. In the SIP model, the coefficient estimates forPrior VWAP returnandPrior SIP MP returnare roughly three times the size for group 5 stocks as they are for group 1 stocks, while the differential is approximately 50X forSIP imbalance. Similar increases in the coefficient estimates also occur with regard to variables in the OL model. Most notably, the coefficient estimates forDifferencemore than doubles in size from approximately 0.222 for group 1 stocks to approximately 0.50 for group 5 stocks.

In addition, the overall model fit differs considerably between the SIP model and the OL model for group 5 stocks. Consistent with columns 1 through 4,R-squared for the SIP model in columns 5 and 7 is a modest .005. In contrast,R-squared increases more than fivefold to .028–.029 for the OL model in columns 6 and 8. While anR-squared of .028 leaves roughly 97% of the variation of VWAP returns unexplained, these results nevertheless highlight a differential in the extent to which prices incorporate the odd-lot quote data relative to the SIP data for higher-priced securities. In the next section, we turn to machine learning to examine whether this differential is sufficient to provide exploitable trading opportunities.

5.3 Machine learning analysis

5.3.1 Model specification

In this section, we use a machine learning (ML) model to examine whether odd-lot quotes predict future stock prices. Relative to structural regression models, ML algorithms have strong predictive properties for two primary reasons. First, these algorithms can fit complex and flexible functional forms to data in a way that can uncover relationships between a set of predictors and outcomes that are not specified in advance. Second, they rely on regularization techniques that avoid over-fitting to maximize out-of-sample predictive performance. In practice, the predictive performance of machine learning is also enhanced by the adoption of a “firewall principle” (Mullainathan and Spiess 2017): none of the data used in fitting the model is used to evaluate the model’s predictive performance. More specifically, prior to fitting the model, the data are split into a “training” data set and a “test” (or hold out) data set. The training data set is used to fit the model and determine all regularization parameters, while the test data set is then used to evaluate the prediction function that is produced.

For our purpose, we employ a supervised machine learning algorithm in which we fit a function to predict whether VWAPt+1is higher than the SIP midpoint att. The particular function we fit usesXGBoost, a gradient boosting algorithm that is closely related to the more common random forest algorithm.XGBoost, however, contains several features that lead to modest improvements in a number of predictive tasks (Chen and Guestrin 2016). As they note,XGBooshas been widely recognized in a number of machine learning and data mining challenges for its accuracy as a stand-alone training algorithm. Likewise, the machine learning competition site Kaggle describesXGBoostas “one of the most popular ML algorithms due to its tendency to yield highly accurate results” (Li 2020). We thus turn to it as a suitable training algorithm for exploring the predictive capacity of odd-lot data.

As inTable 8, we are interested in the predictive capacity of the odd-lot quote data, relative to the SIP quote data, as well as in any differences in their predictive capacity by stock price. Accordingly, we again fit a “SIP model” and an “OL model” using the same parameters for each model utilized inTable 8, and we do this separately for the group 1 data and the group 5 data. We summarize the outcome of interest (or “labels”) and the predictors (or “features”) for the SIP model and the OL model in panel A ofTable 9.27

5.3.2 Model predictions

5.3.2.1 Results

Panel B ofTable 9presents a number of evaluation metrics for the models that were applied to the held-out test data. Evaluating classification models typically requires multiple evaluation metrics in light of a model’s potential to have differentials in the rate of true-positives, true-negatives, false-positives, and false-negatives. This can be illustrated by way of the following confusion matrix for the SIP model for group 1 data:

In the holdout data, the incidence of true “up” minutes was 49.8%. Given the roughly 50/50 split of outcomes,accuracy(i.e., [true positives + true negatives]/all observations) provides a straightforward evaluation metric of the true-positive and true-negative rate. Here, 1,092,792 stock-minutes were correctly predicted to be followed by a VWAPt+1that is higher than the SIP midpoint att(true positives). Likewise, 1,086,764 stock-minutes were correctly predicted not to be followed by a VWAPt+1that is higher than the SIP midpoint att(true negatives). Overall, the model’s accuracy was 58%. This is a remarkably high figure given the poor fit for the SIP model inTable 8, highlighting the classification power of machine learning algorithms. A similar conclusion arises if we focus on the ratio of correctly predicted “up” observations to the total predicted “up” observations (precision) or the ratio of correctly predicted “up” observations to all observations that were actually “up” (recall). Each of these alternative measures was also 58%. The model’s F1 score, which seeks to capture the overall false positive rate and false negative rate, is also 58%, which is not surprising given that the score is calculated as a weighted average of precision and recall.30

We also assess the receiver operator characteristic (ROC) area under the curve (AUC) inFigure 5. ROC is a common evaluation metric for classification models. By default, most classification algorithms determine a class prediction based on whether the predicted probability is greater than 50%. The ROC plot evaluates the true positive rate and the false positive rate of a model using different probability thresholds for a positive classification. A model that perfectly predicted positive cases under any threshold would have an ROC curve that was a vertical line at (0,0) and a horizontal line at (0,1), and the AUC would be 100%. A model that had no predictive capacity would be as a good as a random guess at any threshold, and its ROC curve would be a 45-degree line with an intercept at the origin. The AUC of a model thus provides a measure of the power of the model to correctly classify across all classification thresholds. InFigure 5, panel A, the AUC for the group 1 SIP model is 61%, confirming again the predictive capacity of even the SIP model within the group 1 data.

Having established our primary evaluation criteria, we now examine whether access to the odd-lot quote data improves the model’s predictive accuracy. In panel B ofTable 9, column 2 summarizes the evaluation metrics for the OL model applied to the group 1 data. Overall, the evaluation metrics for the OL model match those of the SIP model, with the exception ofrecallwhich is modestly better for the OL model than for the SIP model. Likewise, panel B ofFigure 5provides the ROC curve and AUC metric for the group 1 OL model, which is also the same as for the group 1 SIP model.

The results are markedly different when we examine the group 5 results in columns 3 and 4 of panel B ofTable 9. Overall, the predictive capacity of both the SIP model and OL model is diminished with these data, which likely reflects the diminished volume of trading and quotation activity for higher priced securities. Nevertheless, the SIP model has anaccuracy,precision, andrecallof 54%, 54%, and 55%, respectively, in test data that has a true positive rate of 50.6%. More importantly, in contrast to the group 1 data, these metrics are all higher in the OL model, where they are 55%, 56%, and 58%, respectively. Likewise, the F1 score and the AUC scores for the OL model are each 3 percentage points higher than for the SIP model (panels C and D ofFigure 5).

5.3.2.2 Feature importance

We use the sHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) method [Lundberg and Lee 2017] to explore why the odd-lot data are driving these performance enhancements within the group 5 data. Originating in game theory, the SHAP method estimates the marginal contribution of each feature in explaining an individual predicted outcome, relative to the expected probability within the sample data. In this regard, the SHAP method is a local explanation method insofar that it explains the individual contributions of a coalition of features (the “players”) to the outcome of an individual prediction (a “game”). For a classification model, a feature’s SHAP value is calculated for each individual observation as the change in the predicted probability of a positive classification due to the addition of that feature, averaged across all possible combinations of the feature values for that observation.

As an illustration, consider the stock-minute for Chipotle depicted inFigure 6, which captures the OL model at the beginning of 09:34:00 on January 8, 2021. According to the OL model, the probability that VWAPt+1would be greater than the SIP midpoint at 09:34:00 was 26%. InFigure 6, this is the “model output value.” Across all stock-minutes in the training data, the expected probability (or the “base vale”) of this occurring was 50.64%; therefore, the OL model was predicting that the observed features caused the predicted probability to be approximately 24.6%lowerthan the expected probability. The SHAP values explain why. In the figure, the light grey and dark grey bars represent features, with the observed value printed below each bar. The dark grey bars represent a decrease in the predicted probability and the light grey bars represent an increase in the predicted probability. The width of each bar represents the feature’s SHAP value for the stock-minute. Thus, the very wide dark grey bar for “difference” (which stretches from approximately 26% to approximately 65%) reflects the fact that its SHAP value was –39.1%, and the slightly narrower light grey bar for “SIP Imbalance” (which stretches from approximately 17% to 26%) reflects a SHAP value of 9.2%. In combination, the aggregate width of all light grey bars is 18.8%, which means that (absent any light grey bars) all of these features would push the predicted probability from 50.64% to approximately 69.4%. Likewise, the aggregate width of all dark grey bars is –43.7%, which means that (absent any light grey bars) all of these features would push the predicted probability down from 50.64% to approximately 6.9%. Overall, the aggregate sum of the SHAP values for light grey and dark grey bars is exactly –24.9%, which explains the predicted probability of 26%.

5.4 Returns

We design a simple trading strategy to examine whether the predictions of the XGBoost algorithm provide potential trading opportunities. Using the held-out test data, we estimate the profits of a round-trip trade that begins with a purchase at the commencement of a stock-minute and ends with a sale at VWAPt+1⁠. Critically, however, we limit trading to those stock-minutes where the model predicts a positive label. We do not consider the symmetric strategy of selling the stock and buying it back when the model predicts a negative label due to the challenges of specifying short sale costs. We note, however, that for some market participants (i.e., market makers) such costs could be negligible, effectively doubling the profitability of any potential trading strategy.

To account for the trading costs, we calculate the mean effective half-spread for all stock-days in the test data by exchange.32The effective half spread is defined as the absolute difference between the trade price and the midpoint of the SIP NBBO at the time of the trade. For each purchase order, we assume a trader must pay the mean effective half-spread for that stock on the exchange having the greatest trading volume for that stock on the day of the trade. To exit the trade, we assume that the trader can place multiple limit orders at timetto sell, a strategy designed to capture the VWAP over the next minute. We approximate this strategy by assuming he or she can exit the trade at VWAPt+1⁠. Where a trade does not occur in the next minute, we assume the trader exits the trade by means of selling at the SIP bid prevailing att+ 1 (thus paying the full spread).

Our initial analysis assumes that a stock is purchased whenever the predicted probability of a positive label is greater than 50%. Note that this strategy effectively assumes that a positive label is predictive of marketable orders to buy over the ensuing 60 seconds that will execute against our (hypothetical) limit orders that are priced at the most aggressive offer prices. As an example, imagine that the SIP NBBO stands at $10.00 × $12.00 when the OL NBBO stands at $10.50 × $12.00 for a minute with a positive label. If effective spreads are $0.15, we would enter the trade at a cost of $11.15 (SIP midpoint + $0.15) and exit the trade by placing aggressive limit orders to sell. If the label were accurate, the VWAPt+1will be higher than the SIP midpoint, meaning that trading during the minute was dominated by marketable orders to buy that execute against resting offers. This example also illustrates why our strategy is biased against finding a profitable trade. While low effective spreads may reflect aggressive odd lots on the offer, this should be inversely related to a positive label (in this example,Differenceis positive at trade initiation, consistent with a positive label, and there were no odd-lot orders to sell). Conversely, to the extent that any trades that compose the VWAPt+1hit very aggressive odd-lot orders to sell, this would suggest our limit orders were priced lower than the SIP best offer, reducing the proceeds we would receive when exiting the trade.

Applying this trading strategy to the group 1 test data would result in approximately 80 buy trades per minute, or 30,000 trades per day between January 4, 2021, and March 31, 2021, for both the SIP model and the OL model. For the group 5 test data, the strategy would result in approximately 19 buy trades per minute, or 7,000 trades per day.

Visual observation of the data reveals that the imposition of trading costs results in negative returns across all models; therefore, purchases were additionally restricted to those stock-minutes that were especially likely to result in a higher VWAPt+1by increasing the probability threshold for a positive label. That is, rather than classify as a positive prediction any stock-minute with a predicted probability of 50% or higher, we incrementally increased the probability threshold for a positive classification to examine whether higher thresholds could yield positive returns to any of the models.

Panel A ofFigure 9shows the results for group 1 stocks. As shown in the figure, increasing the probability threshold results in a sharp decline in the number of trades per day. For instance, at a threshold of 80%, many of the trading days have zero trades for both the SIP model and the OL model, and no trades occur at all at thresholds above 80%. Moreover, across all thresholds, average daily returns are either negative or zero.

Panel A presents returns for group 1 stocks. Panel B presents returns for group 5 stocks. Open in new tab Download slide

Panel B ofFigure 9presents the results for group 5 stocks. As in panel A, total daily trades decline rapidly as we increase the probability threshold for both the SIP model and the OL model. Likewise, the SIP model generally performs poorly. While the SIP model achieves positive average daily returns of 0.053% per day at a threshold of 65% and returns of 0.026% at a threshold of 70%, these returns are generated by just 14 trades per day and 2 trades per day, respectively. At thresholds greater than 75%, the number of daily trades declines to zero.

In contrast, the performance of the OL model is notably stronger for group 5 stocks. In particular, the OL model produces average daily returns of 1.36% at a threshold of 65%, which are generated by approximately 454 trades per day. Moreover, the model results in positive daily trades even when we impose thresholds as high as 90%. The fact that only the OL model produces probabilities of this magnitude is consistent with the greater informational content of odd-lot quotes, especially in higher priced stocks where odd-lot quotes are likely to differ from round-lot quotes.

WhileFigure 9suggests that average returns from the OL model are greatest when the probability threshold is set to 70%, it says little about the risk associated with the trading strategy or the scale at which this strategy is feasible. This risk-return relation can be captured by a Sharpe ratio, which we calculate using the daily returns for the group 5 OL model with a 70% probability threshold.33The average daily return for this model is 2.3%, and the daily volatility is 2.3%. During the sample period the risk-free rate was approximately 10 basis points. In combination, the annualized Sharpe ratio for this trading strategy is therefore a remarkable 15.87.34In contrast, the average daily return and volatility for the group 5 SIP model with a 70% probability threshold are 0.026% and 0.519% (on roughly two trades per day), yielding an annualized Sharpe ratio of 0.79%.

Another metric capturing the risk dimension of this strategy is to consider the frequency of winning and losing days. InFigure 10, we present the distribution of daily returns across the 61 trading days for the SIP model and the OL model for group 5 stocks using a 70% probability threshold. We find that daily returns for the SIP model are zero or negative for 37 of the 61 trading days (61%). In contrast, daily returns for the OL model are zero or negative on just 11 of the 61 trading days (18%), and positive for the remaining 50 days. Moreover, with the exception of a single day where the daily return was roughly –4%, losses across these 11 days ranged from just –0.04% to –1.02%.
