Lastly, with regard to the scale of this strategy, we calculate the total dollar volume of marketable buy orders that arrive for each minute that is assigned with a positive label for the group 5 OL model. For these stock-minutes, the profitability of the trade is largely dependent on the arrival of marketable orders to buy that cause the VWAP to move above the SIP midpoint plus the effective spread. Overall, setting the probability threshold to 70% would result in approximately 10,000 stock-minutes with positive labels across all group 5 stocks during our sample period. Across these stock-minutes, the mean (median) dollar volume of marketable buy-orders was approximately $685,000 ($152,000), while the aggregate dollar volume was nearly $6.5 billion. To be sure, we lack the ability to estimate the scope of initiating trades at or better than the median effective spread given that we cannot observe hidden liquidity. Nevertheless, these figures indicate that the strategy might have considerable scale for those traders capable of initiating trades at or near the SIP midpoint.
article.6 Is Redefining Round Lots the Answer? T:
Our results demonstrate how odd-lot quotes constitute a “market inside the market,” providing advantages to those market participants able to observe and access such quotes. The issues we identify here are part of a larger set of issues falling under the general rubric of the collection, consolidation and dissemination of equity market data. What data should be on the consolidated tape, how should it be recorded, who should provide it, what should it cost? These questions are at the heart of how the U.S. equity marketsshouldoperate. The SEC has reexamined these questions in Reg NMS II, a new regulatory framework that, while adopted, may be years away from implementation (SEC 2020a). Much of this proposed framework is beyond our focus here, but the new rules affecting the new definition of a round lot are certainly in our purview. In this section, we investigate how well the SEC’s proposed definition addresses the problems posed by odd-lot quotes.

As noted earlier, the SEC framework moves away from a standard 100 share round-lot definition applied to all (or all but Berkshire Hathaway and a handful of other stocks) listed equities to a price-dependent metric. For stocks with prices at or below $250.00, a round lot stays at 100 shares; for stock prices greater than $250.00 and up to $1,000.00, a round lot would be 40 shares; for stocks above $1,000.00 up to $10,000.00, 10 shares; and for stocks above that level, 1 share. A second relevant rule change involves the calculation of an exchange’s best bid or offer (BBO). The new approach would allow aggregation of some odd-lot limit orders to influence the BBO and, therefore, the NBBO.

We use a counterfactual approach to investigate how these rule changes would have affected the incidence of odd-lot quotes over our January to March 2021 sample period. To be sure, the quotes we observe during the sample period reflect traders’ behavior under the current regulatory regime, and this behavior might have been different had the new round-lot rules applied. Nonetheless, imposing the new rules on the quotes we observe during the sample period allows us an opportunity to examine how much the new rules would reduce odd-lot quotes in an ideal environment where quoting behavior was orthogonal to the definition of a round lot.

To conduct our counterfactual analysis, we first calculate what would have been the top of book quote for each venue under the new rules. The SEC’s new framework calculates an exchange’s BBO as the least aggressive price at the top of the book that would satisfy all displayed orders in an amount equal to a round lot. To see how this works, we present a simple example inTable 10for Nasdaq. In the table we use the quote data on Nasdaq for AMZN at 13:25:00 on March 31, 2021, which we previously discussed inFigure 2. Under the SEC’s proposal, the new round lot for AMZN on this day would have been 10 shares because its average stock price for the prior month was between $1,000 and $10,000. The order book for AMZN comprises limit orders wishing to buy (the bid side) and limit orders wishing to sell (the ask side).35Looking at the first three columns ofTable 10, we find bids at Nasdaq for varying prices between $3,112.59 down to $3,110.67. Note that the bid at $3,110.67 is for 100 shares and that every other bid is for a smaller amount. The better-priced odd-lot quotes aggregate to only 95 shares; therefore, under Nasdaq’s current aggregation rule, the national best bid (the top of book) for Nasdaq would be priced at the 100-share order at $3,110.67.36Under the new rules, the national best bid can be determined by walking down the book and aggregating the orders until they constitute at least the new round-lot level of 10. Starting at $3,112.59, we reach this level at $3,111.81. The top of book for Nasdaq would thus be a bid price of $3,111.81 with a depth of 23 shares, as shown in columns 4 and 5.

Given these SEC changes, the “inside market” will be reduced, but with only 4 round-lot categories the effects will differ depending upon a stock’s price. To determine this, we calculated how this approach would have affected the incidence of better odd-lot prices for the 1,736 sample stocks using data from January to March 2021. Under the new rules, a stock’s round-lot definition will vary based on its prior month’s stock price; therefore, we assign stocks to a round-lot category on a month-by-month basis. For each of the three months, an average of 1,612 stocks retained the 100-share round-lot level, 108 would have a 40-share round lot, and 8.3 would have a 10-share round lot (no stock in our sample would have a 1 stock round lot). For each stock, we next calculate each exchange’s BBO using the new round-lot definitions for each stock, and we calculate the new NBBO by taking the best BBO from across all exchanges. Lastly, we evaluate all end of minute quotes as to whether an odd-lot bid (ask) is superior to this new NBBO. The incidence of better odd-lot pricing is the average percent of these end of minute observations for each stock.

Table 11provides results on the incidence of better odd-lot pricing relative to the NBBO with the new definitions and the old. As expected, there is no change for the stocks in panel A that faced no change in the round-lot definition. This is not the case in panel B (40 share round lot), where the incidence of better odd-lot pricing falls an average of 4.9% for bids and 4.9% for offers or in panel C, where it falls 26.7% for bids and 25.0% for offers. By this simple metric, the new policy has reduced the inside market. However, in both categories the incidence of better odd-lot prices remains substantial. For the 40-share category, 35.5% (33.5%) of the time there exists a better bid (ask) than the NBBO price; the 10-share category has a better bid 37.0% of the time and a better ask 35.3% of the time. The SEC’s approach of redefining what constitutes a round lot appears to make headway on the inside market issue, but a substantial problem remains.

article.7 Conclusions:
What determines the “best” trading price available in the market, how limit orders for small quantities fit into the quote montage, and who gets to see this information are all issues that greatly influence the efficiency and fairness of the U.S. equity markets. We find that current market practices relating to odd-lot quotes result in a large “inside” market where for many stocks better prices routinely exist relative to the NBBO. We provide strong evidence that being able to see these odd-lot quotes provides valuable information to traders with access to proprietary data feeds. We also show that the SEC’s approach of changing the definition of a round lot reduces, but does not ameliorate, the high incidence of superior odd-lot quotes within the NBBO.

What then to do about odd-lot quotes? One way to think about this question is to return to the basic issue of what markets actually do and how SEC regulation should relate to this. At a basic level, markets provide liquidity and price discovery, and our results show that odd-lot quotes play an important role in both of these functions. To facilitate these market functions, the National Market System was structured to meet five goals: promote fair market competition between participants, efficient order execution, transparency of price quotes, best price execution, and direct matching of buy and sell orders.37As our results show, the rising importance of odd-lot quotes interferes with meeting virtually all of these goals, suggesting that odd-lot quotes must be explicitly considered in the National Market System.

One approach is to simply make odd-lot quotation data cheaper to acquire. This is effectively the approach taken in the proposed Reg NMS II, which seeks to promote a competitive market for exchange data. In particular, odd-lot quotes that remain despite the new round-lot definitions will be part of “core” data that exchanges must distribute to “competing consolidators.” As envisioned by Reg NMS II, these new market participants will compete in the offering of market data products, such as by providing a lower-priced product that resembles the current SIP quote data (e.g., a product that provides simply the exchange BBOs) as well as more expensive products that include more elaborate quote data, such as odd lots and depth of book data. In this regard, Reg NMS II represents a continuation of a “market inside a market,” particularly for higher-priced stocks, where our results show that odd-lot quotes will continue to be substantial, and only traders who buy the more expensive data products will see them.

If the policy objective is to level the playing field—that is, to meet the fairness, efficiency, and transparency objectives noted above—then our analysis suggests refining the round-lot definitions by relying on machine learning. In particular, it would seem that the optimal round lot is one in which knowing the remaining odd-lot data provide little or no incremental value to market participants. Machine learning-based analysis, rather thanad hoccharacterizations, would better achieve this goal. Another direction to reduce the asymmetry may be to shrink the size of the inside market by changing the minimum tick size for higher priced stocks. Indeed, even after the SEC’s 2016 tick size pilot program, proposals to modify the current one-penny minimum tick size continue to circulate.38In principle, modifying the tick size could be done in such a way as to reduce the number of odd lot quotes by shrinking the possible quote price points. While this may prove a useful construct, the tick size pilot (which was generally viewed as a failure) suggests that varying tick levels across stocks can be problematic.

Perhaps the biggest puzzle is why firms do not solve this problem themselves by splitting their stocks to lower price levels. While some firms (Tesla, Amazon, Alphabet) initiated stock splits in 2022, there remain numerous stocks with high price levels.39Weld et al. (2020)found that since the Great Depression the average price for a share of stock on the NYSE remained roughly constant at $35.00. A variety of changes in the market, such as decimal trading, smaller ticks, and the reduction of commissions, have dramatically reduced transaction costs and so may have changed the “optimal” price level, but our results suggest these changes have not made the price level irrelevant. Suggestions that fractional share trading solves the high stock price problem for retail traders, or that most stocks are held indirectly in mutual funds and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) managed by institutional institutions who do not care about high price levels miss an important point: high priced stocks provide profit opportunities for high frequency and other sophisticated traders who know, and profit from, the inside market. A return to lower stock price levels would alleviate this problem.
